coredata.dc:title: N doped ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers as visible light driven photocatalyst for the degradation of malachite green dye in aqueous phase
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Shelja
Mehta, S.K.
Kansal, S.K.
coredata.dc:description: In an attempt to fabricate potent visible light driven photocatalyst, we have synthesized N-ZnO/C-dots via facile hydrothermal technique. The prepared N-ZnO/C-dots sample was characterized in detail by X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Fourier transmission infra-red spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), UV-diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The results confirmed the presence of nitrogen and successfully introduction of C-dots onto the surface of the N-ZnO. The photocatalytic activity of the prepared nanoflowers was investigated by photocatalytic decomposition of MG dye under visible light. About 85% of MG dye was degraded with N-ZnO/C-dots, higher than that of N-ZnO (75%), pure ZnO (65%) and other commercially available photocatalysts (TiO2 PC 50 (20%) and PC 500 (30%)) under visible light irradiation. The as synthesized N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers also exhibited excellent visible light driven photocatalysis towards levofloxacin drug. The effect of operational parameters such as initial dye concentration, pH and catalyst dose on degradation was also studied. The photocatalytic degradation of MG dye was found to be fit with pseudo first order kinetics. Scavenger studies were carried out to investigate the role of reactive radical species in photocatalytic process. The improved photocatalytic activity was accredited to the adequate separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons and holes, which is accountable to the presence of C-dots as it served as a main role in accepting the photogenerated electrons.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-ZnO/C-dots
Visible light
Hydrothermal method
Photocatalysis
Malachite green

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped zinc oxide (N-ZnO)
Synthesis of nitrogen doped zinc oxide/carbon dots (N-ZnO/C-dots)
Structural, morphological and compositional properties of N-ZnO and N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers
Investigation of photocatalytic activity of N-ZnO and N-ZnO/C-dots by degradation of MG dye under visible light
Plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye using N- ZnO/C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical XRD spectrum of (a) as synthesized N-ZnO and (b) N-ZnO/C-dots.
FESEM images of (a) and (b) N-ZnO (c) and (d) N-ZnO/C-dots at different magnifications.
HRTEM images of N-ZnO/C-dots (a) at low magnification (b) at high magnification.
(a) FTIR spectra of N-ZnO and N-ZnO/C-dots (b) TGA curve of N-ZnO/C-dots (c) Room temperature PL spectra (D) Kubelka-Munk transformed reflectance spectra of N-ZnO and N-ZnO/C-dots.
(a) Wide Scan XPS spectrum of N-ZnO/C-dots. High resolution spectra of (b) Zn 2p (c) N 1s (d) C 1s (e) O 1s.

(a) Absorbance spectra of MG dye at various time intervals (20 mg/L, pH 5, N-ZnO/C-dots dose 0.05 g/L) (b) Comparison of photolysis and photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of ZnO, N-ZnO, N-ZnO/C-dots and commercial catalysts PC 50 and PC 500 (20 mg/L, pH 5, catalyst dose 0.05 g/L). Effect of (c) pH and (d) catalyst dose on the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye.
(a) Effect of initial dye concentration on the degradation efficiency of N-ZnO/C-dots (b) Role of scavengers on the degradation efficiency of N-ZnO/C-dots.
Plot of ln (C0/C) versus time displaying the photocatalytic decomposition of MG dye.
Time dependent photocatalytic degradation of levofloxacin using N-ZnO/C-dots (catalyst dose 0.1 g/L, pH natural, levofloxacin 10 mg/L).
Diagrammatic representation of mechanism for photocatalysis of N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers.
Kinetics data of MG dye photodegradation in the presence of different photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: N doped ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers as visible light driven photocatalyst for the degradation of malachite green dye in aqueous phase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In an attempt to fabricate potent visible light driven photocatalyst, we have synthesized N-ZnO/C-dots via facile hydrothermal technique. The prepared N-ZnO/C-dots sample was characterized in detail by X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Fourier transmission infra-red spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), UV-diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The results confirmed the presence of nitrogen and successfully introduction of C-dots onto the surface of the N-ZnO. The photocatalytic activity of the prepared nanoflowers was investigated by photocatalytic decomposition of MG dye under visible light. About 85% of MG dye was degraded with N-ZnO/C-dots, higher than that of N-ZnO (75%), pure ZnO (65%) and other commercially available photocatalysts (TiO2 PC 50 (20%) and PC 500 (30%)) under visible light irradiation. The as synthesized N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers also exhibited excellent visible light driven photocatalysis towards levofloxacin drug. The effect of operational parameters such as initial dye concentration, pH and catalyst dose on degradation was also studied. The photocatalytic degradation of MG dye was found to be fit with pseudo first order kinetics. Scavenger studies were carried out to investigate the role of reactive radical species in photocatalytic process. The improved photocatalytic activity was accredited to the adequate separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons and holes, which is accountable to the presence of C-dots as it served as a main role in accepting the photogenerated electrons.

ce:section.ce:para: Heterogeneous photocatalysis is an environment friendly and economical approach for the degradation of organic pollutants persisting in our environment. This technique has been widely used for the mineralization of organic contaminants through a sequence of various intermediates into inorganic compounds such as water, carbon dioxide and others so on [1]. Semiconductor photocatalysis seems to be an emergent and promising technology and has number of applications in environmental remediation such as purification of water and air along with other hazardous waste [2]. Semiconductor based photocatalysts such as TiO2, ZnO, Bi2S3, Fe2O3 etc have been investigated in detail [3–7]. However, the wide band gap of these semiconductors restricts its applicability to longer wavelength region. Photocatalysts that are visible light active are immensely important and enviable for the decomposition of toxic organic pollutants [8,9]. In this context, Zinc oxide (ZnO), a direct and wide gap semiconductor (3.37 eV) is one of most studied photocatalyst due to its unique electro-optic properties, low cost, physical and chemical stability, high catalytic activity, environmental friendliness and ease of availability [10]. Also, the morphologies of ZnO are quite controlled, which is a key factor and can influence its photocatalytic performance [11]. But ZnO is found to be photoactive only in the ultraviolet region, which consists only 4–5% of freely available solar light [12]. Keeping in view, its lower photocatalytic activity under visible light, various strategies have been employed to enhance the properties and catalytic activity of ZnO, such as by doping with transition metals or non-metals, modification with metals and carbon based nanoparticles, coupling with second generation semiconductors [13–16]. Thus the fabrication of ZnO with other efficient materials presents new approaches and offer novel properties in the field of photocatalysis. Sanchez et al. synthesized nitrogen doped ZnO by sol-gel method and evaluated its photocatalytic activity for degradation of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid and picrolam [17]. Lamba et al. reported the synthesis of CeO2- ZnO hexagonal nanodisks and carried out the efficient photocatalytic degradation of direct blue dye under solar light irradiation [18]. High quality p-type nitrogen doped ZnO nanostructures having controlled morphology were synthesized by Herring et al. using microwave assisted method [19]. Gautam et al. demonstrated the synthesis of pure and nitrogen doped ZnO nanobullets by solvothermal method and studied its cathodoluminescence and field emission properties [20]. Peng et al. modified ZnO nanosheets with BiVO4 and demonstrated its enhanced visible photocatalytic activity for the degradation of rhodamine B dye [21].

Carbon quantum dots (C-dots), a new class of multicolor luminescent carbon nanomaterials, having sizes almost less than 10 nm, have been of great interest due to its broad absorption spectrum, large absorption coefficient, upconversion luminescence and photoinduced electron transfer property [22–24]. C-dots act as electron reservoir and improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers and enhance the photocatalytic activity [25]. Yao et al. designed TiO2/C-dots and SiO2/C-dots nanocomposite photocatalysts via a sol-gel method for the degradation of methylene blue dye [26]. A number of C-dots based photocatalysts with enhanced catalytic activity such as C-dots/Fe2O3, C-dots/Ag/Ag3PO4, C-dots/Au(Pt) have been reported [27,28]. The design of such system cause great suppression to the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and extend the spectral response to longer wavelengths. Based on the above studies, the combination of ZnO and C-dots seems to be an ideal strategy for enhancing the photocatalytic activity under visible light [29].

In the present work, we have synthesized nitrogen doped ZnO/C-dots (N-ZnO/C-dots) via facile low temperature hydrothermal method and characterized with various analytical and spectroscopic techniques. The prepared N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers were used as an efficient visible light induced photocatalyst for the degradation of malachite green (MG) dye in an aqueous phase. Experimental results suggest that the photoinduced electron transfer property of C-dots played a pivotal role in the enhanced photocatalytic activity of N-ZnO/C-dots.

Zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 98.0%), ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.0%), isopropanol (IPA; C3H8O, 99.0%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.0%) and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 99.0%) were purchased from Merck, India. Liquid Ammonia (NH4OH, 99.0%) was procured from Qualigens Fine Chemicals, India. L-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, 99.0%) was purchased from Himedia, India. TiO2 PC 50 (99.0%) and PC 500 (99.0%) were received as gift from Millennium Chemicals, France. All the chemicals used are of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Double distilled water has been used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped zinc oxide (N-ZnO)
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthesis procedure, 2.5 g of zinc acetate dihydrate was dissolved in 100 mL of water. The ammonia solution was added dropwise to adjust the pH value of above solution to 10. The obtained turbid solution was further diluted with 50 mL of water. The whole solution was then transferred to 150 mL teflon lined stainless steel autoclave in an oven at 110 °C for 19 h. The autoclave was cooled down naturally to room temperature. The obtained sol was taken out and washed with mixture of water and ethanol. It was allowed to stand for some time. The supernatant was discarded and precipitates were thoroughly washed with alcoholic mixture for several times. The precipitates were then kept in oven at 50 °C overnight for drying.

C-dots are synthesized by one pot hydrothermal method. 0.25 g of ascorbic acid was added to 50 mL ethanol. To this reaction mixture, 20 mL of water was added. The resulting solution was kept under stirring for 10 min. Further, it was transferred to Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and kept in oven for 3 h at 160 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped zinc oxide/carbon dots (N-ZnO/C-dots)
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of N-ZnO/C-dots photocatalyst, a specific amount of C-dots (50 μL) was mixed with 100 mg of N-ZnO crystals suspended in 50 mL of ethanol and stirred for 1 h at room temperature. It was then filtered and washed with distilled water several times and dried overnight.

The crystal structure and phase purity of the as-prepared catalysts were analyzed with X-ray diffraction (XRD) using X-ray diffraction (XRD; PANanalyticalXpert Pro.), measured with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54056 Å)at 45 kV and 40 mA by scanning at a rate of 2°(2θ) min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with AMICUS, Kratos Analytical (Shimadzu), spectroscope. The binding energy in XPS analysis was corrected by the C (1s) adventitious peak. The general morphology of the synthesized nanoparticles was analyzed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), using FEI, Tecnai F 20, Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), using HITACHI, SU8010 equipped with energy dispersive spectroscope (EDX) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. The UV–vis absorption studies of the photocatalysts were conducted on UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2450) using BaSO4as a reference. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed by using a Thermoscientific (Nicolet iS50) FTIR spectrometer, equipped with a diamond ATR at room temperature in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The Nicolet design allows achieving a wavelength precision and accuracy better than 0.01 cm−1. The room temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (5J1-004 model) using quartz cuvette 1 cm path length at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm.

The photocatalytic activity of prepared catalysts was determined by examining the photocatalytic degradation of malachite green (MG) dye under visible light irradiation (Philips bulb 150 W, Intensity 2150 lm and a wavelength range of 400–520 nm) at constant temperature with water circulator. In a photocatalytic experiment, the model contaminant, MG dye (20 mg/L aqueous solution) was subjected to photocatalytic degradation. The reaction conditions were also varied so as to optimize the catalyst dose and pH. A certain dose of photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL of MG dye in a double walled vessel under stirring. Prior to illumination, the resulting solution was kept in dark for half an hour to ensure the achievement of adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Further, the photocatalytic experiment was initiated by illuminating the system with visible light and after specific time intervals; certain aliquots of solution were withdrawn out with syringe and made to pass through 0.45 μm Chromafil filter. The MG concentrations were monitored at λ max = 617 nm during the photocatalytic process using UV–vis spectrophotometer and its degradation efficiency was calculated by
Degradation
efficiency
( % )
=
[
(
C 0
−
C )
/
C 0
]
×
100
where C0 and C are the concentrations of MG dye before and after the visible light illuminations.

Moreover, the role of reactive species was also investigated by using different scavengers (IPA, NaCl and K2Cr2O7). For this study, 0.01 M of scavengers was introduced into the dye solution prior to the addition of photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, morphological and compositional properties of N-ZnO and N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para:
The crystal structure, phase and purity of prepared photocatalysts were determined by XRD analysis. Fig. 1 (a) displays typical XRD pattern of N- ZnO exhibiting several well defined diffraction patterns. The characteristic diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ = 31.76°, 34.42°, 36.28°, 47.56°, 56.60°, 62.90°, 66.35°, 67.94°, 69.0°, 72.58° and 76.95° could be corresponded as (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004) and (202) respectively and were associated to wurtzite hexagonal phase of ZnO (JCPDS No. 36–1451) [30]. These obtained diffraction peaks were in well consistent with the reported literature. No other characteristic peaks for impurity or ZnO in other phases were observed, suggesting the high purity of the prepared nanoflowers. No another peak due to nitrogen was observed in any of the spectra and hence the occurrence of residual nitrogen or compound/complex of nitrogen, was also ruled out for prepared N doped ZnO materials [31]. Also, the narrow and strong diffraction peaks demonstrated the high crystallinity of the as prepared nanoflowers [32]. It could be observed that the introduction of nitrogen atoms did not affect the ZnO crystal structure [17]. The crystallite size of the catalyst was calculated using Debye-Scherrer's equation as mentioned below:
D =
K λ /
β cos θ
where K is a constant, λ is the wavelength i.e. 1.54056 Å, β is the full width at half maxima (FWHM) of a diffracted peak and θ is the Bragg angle. The average crystallite size was found to be ∼51.2 nm with accuracy in measurement of 0.077%.

The spacing between the adjacent planes (dhkl) in atomic lattice can be calculated from bragg's equation as mentioned below:
2 d
hkl
sin θ = n
λ
where n is the integer, θ is the angle between incident ray and scattering planes. For hexagonal phase, the following equation was used to determine lattice parameters “a” and “c”:
1 /
(
d
hkl
)
2
=
4 / 3
[
(
h 2
+ hk
+
k
2
)
/a
2
]
+
l 2
/c
2
where h, k, l are miller indices. The lattice parameters “a” and “c” were calculated to be 3.2504 Å and 5.2057 Å [33]. The standard lattice parameters for pure ZnO by Rietveld refinement are a = 3.2621 Å and c = 5.2184 Å [34]. The decrease in values of lattice parameters from that of pure ZnO indicated the successful doping of nitrogen into ZnO lattice. Furthermore, in Fig. 1(b), in addition to the characteristic peaks of wurtzite hexagonal phase of ZnO, interestingly, a broad peak at 24° was observed which could be related to the minor amount of C-dots in the prepared catalyst [35].

The morphologies and structures of N- ZnO and N- ZnO/C-dots were investigated by FESEM and HRTEM. Fig. 2 shows the FESEM images of the synthesized catalysts. It can be seen in Fig. 2(a), that for N- ZnO, large scale flower like nanostructures were obtained. From the enlarged image (Fig. 2(b)), in the individual flower, there was accumulation of many horizontal and vertical nanorods which aligned themselves into the form of flowers. However, upon the addition of C-dots, the sharpen edges of nanorods became somewhat smoother, resulted in the little distortion of flowers (Fig. 2(c and d)). The similar kind of morphology was also seen from HRTEM images, shown in Fig. 3 . At low magnification, N-ZnO/C-dots displayed nanoflower like morphology and under high resolution observation, we could identify C-dots (light spots) with irregular lattice uniformly distributed over N-ZnO nanorods [36]. The elemental composition of the N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers was examined using energy dispersive X-ray analysis. The EDX pattern of N-ZnO/C-dots displayed the existence of nitrogen with Zn, O and C thereby, confirming the successful doping of nitrogen into ZnO matrix (Fig. 4 ). It also indicated that the nanostructures were consisting of mixed carbon materials and N-ZnO nanoflowers.

The FTIR analyses were carried out to determine the interaction between N- ZnO and C-dots (Fig. 5 (a)). The broad absorption band centered at 3361 cm−1 and peaks at 870 cm−1 and 675 cm−1 were associated with stretching band vibrations of hydrogen bonded O-H group, OH twisting vibrations, related to substitutional hydrogen at oxygen site HO bond to the lattice Zn site (Zn-HO) and characteristic peak of Zn-O, respectively [37,38]. In FTIR spectrum of N-ZnO/C-dots, few more characteristic peaks were observed in addition to N-ZnO. The peaks at 2920, 1645 and 1396 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C- H, CO and O-C-O (epoxy) group, respectively [39]. Considering the errors, the FTIR results showed 0.64% accuracy in measurement. The FTIR results provided a further support that C-dots have been successfully attached to the ZnO nanoparticles, which was in agreement with the XRD results.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is generally used in order to characterize thermal stability of prepared catalysts as shown in Fig. 5(b). The TGA curve illustrated two minor weight losses for N-ZnO/C-dots. The as prepared sample showed very small (0.8%) weight loss at the temperature up to 200 °C, which is most likely due to the loss of adsorbed water molecules over the surface. Another weight loss (3.5%) was observed between the temperature regions 200 °C–680 °C, which was ascribed to the decomposition of oxygen containing functional groups. Such minor weight loss of the prepared catalyst exhibited the high thermal stability over the entire temperature range.

Fig. 5(c) represents room temperature PL spectra of as synthesized N-ZnO and N-ZnO/C-dots at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. N-ZnO displayed a near band edge emission at 335 nm, which demonstrated the excitonic recombination of charge carriers [40]. But in N-ZnO/C-dots, the intensity of the emission band decreased significantly when the C-dots is introduced onto N-ZnO as compared to the bare one, implying that the N-ZnO/C-dots could effectively decrease the recombination of the photogenerated electrons and holes pairs [41]. However, no emission band was observed in the region of 500–600 nm wavelength region, which was mainly due to the surface defects indicated the purity of as synthesized N-ZnO. In the case of N-ZnO/C-dots, a feeble characteristic emission band at 400 nm was observed, which could be assigned to the surface states of C-dots. The PL spectra of the N-ZnO and N-ZnO/C-dots displayed the same lineshape, however, the emission intensity varied, which revealed that effective interaction between N-ZnO and C-dots occurred in the hybrid system [42].

In order to investigate the optical absorption characteristics of the prepared catalysts, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopic studies were carried out using Tauc's plot. The band gap of pure ZnO was reported to be 3.37 eV. Fig. 5(d) shows the Kubelka–Munk transformed reflectance spectra of N-ZnO/C-dots and N-ZnO. Using Kubelka-Munk function, the (F(R)hυ)2 was plotted against hυ. It was observed that N-ZnO/C-dots and N-ZnO showed band gap of 2.75 eV and 2.98 eV respectively [17]. The reduction of band gap value of N-ZnO from pure ZnO confirmed the doping of nitrogen with ZnO. While, a lower value was detected for N-ZnO/C-dots i.e. 2.75 eV, indicating that the wider absorption capability of N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers [43]. Hence, it can be recommended to utilize prepared nanocomposite as an effective photocatalyst even in the visible region.

XPS was used to investigate the surface chemical composition of N-ZnO/C-dots. The binding energies have been calibrated by taking the carbon C 1s peak (287.6 eV) as reference. Fig. 6 (a) shows the wide XPS survey spectrum of N-ZnO/C-dots and Fig. 6(b)-(e) displays the high resolution spectra of other specific constituent elements. From Fig. 6(a) it is clear that the catalyst is composed of Zn, O, N and C. In the high resolution spectrum of Zn 2p, the Zn 2p3/2and Zn 2p1/2 spin–orbital splitting photoelectrons for the sample was found to be located at binding energies of 1022.1 eV and 1044.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 6(b)) [11]. The existence of nitrogen was further confirmed by XPS (Fig. 6(c)). In the high resolution spectrum of N 1s, it could be seen that a peak centered at 398.9 eV for the doped sample is obtained, which could be ascribed to the anionic N− in the form of Zn-N bonds [44]. Thus the XPS data supported the incorporation of N into the ZnO. Although the peak in the XPS survey profile was weak, the peak position and its broadening were clear in the N 1s scan profile. As shown in Fig. 6(e) the well-defined peak observed at the binding energy of 531 eV corresponds to the oxygen atom coordinated with ZnO and oxygen bound to C atom [11]. Thus, based on mentioned XRD, XPS and EDX analysis it can be reasonable to deduce that the N-doped ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of photocatalytic activity of N-ZnO and N-ZnO/C-dots by degradation of MG dye under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activities of prepared catalysts were investigated using MG dye as a model contaminant. MG dye is widely used in aquaculture as a parasiticide and in food, health, textile and other industries. It has been reported to cause carcinogenesis, mutagenesis, chromosomal fractures, teratogenicity and respiratory toxicity. It exhibits absorption peaks 424 nm and 617 nm [45]. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the absorbance of the MG dye was getting diminished and 85% of the dye degraded within 160 min of visible light irradiation in presence of N-ZnO/C-dots. It was found that no degradation of MG dye took place under visible light in the absence of photocatalyst. Hence, photolytic process is inefficient for the degradation of MG dye.

The photocatalytic activities of the prepared catalysts have also been compared with commercially available photocatalysts i.e. TiO2 PC 500 and TiO2 PC 50 with catalyst dose 0.05 g/L, concentration of dye 20 mg/L at pH 5 (Fig. 7(b)). The photocatalytic degradation of MG dye in the presence of different photocatalysts under visible light in 160 min was found to be: TiO2 PC 50 (20%), TiO2 PC 500 (30%), ZnO (65%), N- ZnO (75%) and N-ZnO/C-dots (85%). The maximum degradation was achieved using N-ZnO/C-dots as photocatalyst under visible light irradiation. Hence, the as prepared N-ZnO/C-dots exhibited highest photocatalytic activity and was found to be highly efficient as compared to other commercially available catalysts i.e. PC 50 and PC 500, the benchmark TiO2 catalysts, for the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye in aqueous phase under visible light irradiation. The combinational effect of nitrogen doping and the C-dots, both make ZnO to outperform the commercial catalyst in their photocatalytic activity, which is of great importance for the commercialization of nanotechnology.

The most important parameters such as catalyst loading, initial dye concentration and pH variation on the photocatalytic degradation of MG were also investigated. The influence of pH on the photocatalytic activity of N-ZnO/C-dots was studied. As shown in Fig. 7(c), it indicates the optimum pH for photocatalytic degradation of MG dye is 5. Keeping catalyst dose (0.05 g/L) and concentration of dye (20 mg/L) fixed, it was observed that with increasing pH from 3 to 5, the photocatalytic degradation rate of MG dye greatly increased from 42% to 85%. As the pH was further increased from 5 to higher value, the photocatalytic degradation rate decreased to 63%. This is due to the influence of pH on the surface-charge of catalyst and the ionic species of the MG dye in the solution.

In order to determine the effect of the catalyst dosage on the photocatalytic degradation of 100 mL of 20 mg/L MG dye aqueous solution, different amounts of the prepared catalyst (N-ZnO/C-dots) in the range of 0.025 g/L - 0.075 g/L were used at constant pH 5. As shown in Fig. 7(d), by increasing the amount of photocatalyst from 0.025 g/L to 0.05 g/L the photocatalytic degradation efficiency increased from 70% to 85%, which was due to the enhancement in number of active sites present on the surface of photocatalyst, whereas further increase in concentration of catalyst from 0.05 g/L to 0.075 g/L, there was decrease in photocatalytic degradation of MG dye to 82%, due to increase in the turbidity of the solution which obstructed the penetration of light to the dye solution. Also, the agglomeration of nanoparticles because of particle–particle interactions increases for a large dosage of nanocomposite results in a reduction of surface area of photocatalyst, available for absorption of light which led to decrease in the photocatalytic degradation rate. Hence, the optimum catalyst dose for the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye was found to be 0.05 g/L.

Fig. 8 (a) shows the effect of initial dye concentration on the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye using N-ZnO/C-dots keeping other parameters constant i.e. pH = 5 and catalyst dose = 0.05 g/L. There was insignificant change observed in the degradation efficiency of the catalyst when the concentration of dye was increased from 10 mg/L to 20 mg/L. On further increasing the initial dye concentration to 30 mg/L, the degradation efficiency was found to be decreased. As a given amount of adsorbent has capacity to adsorb some fixed amount of adsorbate species. And because of adsorption of more and more organic species on the surface of N-ZnO/C-dots with lower substrate initial concentration, the degradation efficiency decreased.

Scavenger studies were also carried out to determine the participation of active radical species responsible for the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye (Fig. 8(b)). IPA (scavenger for ·OH), NaCl (scavenger for h+), K2Cr2O7 (scavenger for e−) have been used. The photocatalytic decomposition of MG dye in the absence of any scavenger was observed up to 85% which was reduced considerably upon addition of IPA, NaCl and K2Cr2O7 to 70%, 65% and 7% only. It indicated that ·OH, e− and h+ played an important role in the degradation process of MG dye under visible light, but electrons were found to be the most dominating species in the photocatalytic degradation system of MG dye.

The reaction kinetics of photocatalytic decomposition of MG dye using N-ZnO/C-dots, N-ZnO and ZnO were also investigated using Langmuir- Hinshelwood kinetic model whose equation is written below:
ln
(
C 0
/ C
)
= kt
where C0 and C are the concentrations of MG dye solutions before and after the visible light irradiations, k is the apparent rate constant which is obtained from slope and t is the time duration of the photocatalytic reaction. Fig. 9 displays the plot of ln (C0/C) vs time. According to the equation, it was observed that photocatalytic degradation of MG dye followed pseudo first order kinetics. The kinetics data is also summarized in Table 1 .

Further photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out using prepared catalyst, N-ZnO/C-dots on another class of hazardous organic compound, i.e. levofloxacin drug. Levofloxacin belongs to the class of fluoroquinolone antibiotics and is widely used as an antibacterial agent, but its resistance against biological degradation leads to potential ecological risks. Fig. 10 shows the UV–vis absorbance spectra of levofloxacin at different intervals of time and it was found that the absorption maxima decreased subsequently with time. The photocatalytic degradation rate was found to be 77% in 160 min of visible light illumination. These findings showed that as synthesized catalyst, N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers can serve as an efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of harmful organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye using N- ZnO/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The enhanced photocatalytic activity of N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers than N-ZnO and other commercially available photocatalysts simply indicates the crucial role of C-dots in the photocatalytic system. The interaction between N-ZnO and C-dots by chelation and complexation can tremendously improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers and increase the migration ratio of electrons and holes pairs [46]. This enhancement can be attributed to the efficient charge separation due to the quick photo-induced charge transfer property of C-dots, which suppress the recombination of electron and hole pairs, also confirmed by PL and UV–vis DRS studies. This efficient charge separation process can boost the lifetime of the charge carriers and this hindered recombination of electron and hole pairs will result to the improved interfacial charge transfer to the adsorbed dye molecules, ultimately increase the number of carriers adsorbed by MG dye. In the present work, the mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye using N-ZnO/C-dots under visible light is proposed as follows:
N − ZnO/C - dots
+
h υ
e −
( C - dots )
+
h +
( VB )
e −
( C - dots )
+
O
2
O
2 ·
h +
( VB )
+
H 2
O
H +
+ ·
OH
2 ·
OH ·
H 2
O 2
H 2
O 2
+
O
2
· −
− ·
OH +
OH
−
+
O 2
MG
Dye
+ ·
OH/
OH
−
CO
2
+
H
2
O

When the N-ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite is irradiated by the visible light, the electrons (e−) in the valence band (VB) of N-ZnO, upon excitation moved to the conduction band (CB) of N-ZnO, and due to the photo-induced charge transfer property of C-dots, electrons (e−) moved freely to the surface of C-dots. Subsequently holes (h+) were left behind in the VB of N-ZnO. The band positions of N-ZnO/C-dots have been calculated by the following empirical equation:
E
VB
=
X − Ee
+
0
. 5 E
g
where, Evb is the valence band edge potential, X is the electronegativity of the semiconductor, Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV), Eg is the corresponding energy band gap of N-ZnO/C-dots which is 2.75 eV. EVB and ECB were calculated as 2.6 eV and −0.3 eV, respectively. Usually, these photogenerated electrons and holes recombine quickly and then only few charge carriers left which take part in the photocatalytic system. However, due to the C-dots, this undesirable recombination process didn't take place. The holes (h+) migrated to the surface of N-ZnO and reacted with adsorbed water (H2O) molecules to produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH). Further, the photo-generated electrons (e−) formed superoxide radicals (O2 ·
−
) on reacting with the adsorbed oxygen on the surface of C-dots, which on protonation, ultimately produced more number of hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which are highly reactive specie to degrade organic pollutant. The diagrammatic representation of the mechanism for the photocatalytic activity of the N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers is shown below in Fig. 11 :

We have synthesized N-ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite by a simple hydrothermal technique. The successful doping of nitrogen and introduction of C-dots on the surface of N-ZnO has been confirmed by various analytical techniques. N-ZnO/C-dots nanoflowers exhibited higher photocatalytic efficiency (85%) than N- ZnO (75%), ZnO (65%) and commercial photocatalysts such as TiO2 PC 500 (30%) and TiO2 PC 50 (20%) for degradation of MG dye under visible light and similar operating parameters. Additionally, the prepared nanoflowers also exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity in degrading fluoroquinolone antibiotic, i.e. levofloxacin drug. The improved photocatalytic activity of N-ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite was ascribed to the separation efficiency of charge carriers and the obstructed recombination of electron and hole pairs because of the existing interactions between N-ZnO and C-dots. Electrons, holes and hydroxyl radicals were found to be highly desirable reactive species for the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye. The plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic activity of N-ZnO/C-dots was also proposed and the reaction kinetics was following pseudo first order kinetics. These results gain deep insight into the interactions of C-dots with photocatalysts, and also provide a new approach to develop superior and improved photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the financial support received under the UGC – BSR Government of India through a grant No. F. 25-1/2013(BSR)/5- 91/2007 (BSR) and TEQIP-II grant of Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar UICET, Panjab University, Chandigarh. The authors would also like to thank SAIF, Panjab University, Chandigarh for providing required instrumentation facility.
